cases.title: Hyland v. Chicago Metro Presbytery
cases.synopsis: A terminated church employee filed a complaint against Chicago Metro Presbytery after Presbytery declined to indict the pastors she accused, but Presbytery ruled her complaint out of order for lack of standing under BCO 43-1. The SJC, voting 12-12, left the Presbytery's decision standing, finding the complaint judicially out of order because the complainant lacked standing to file a BCO 43-1 complaint against Presbytery.

cases.title: Flatgard v. Metro Atlanta Presbytery
cases.synopsis: TE Flatgard was deposed by Metro Atlanta Presbytery on three charges related to conduct during his marital separation and divorce; the SJC sustained his appeal 14-3, finding the Record insufficient to support a guilty verdict and censure on the charges of failing to maintain elder qualifications, failing to love his wife, and failing to uphold ordination vows. The case generated extensive concurring and dissenting opinions debating appellate deference standards under BCO 39-3.2, 39-3.3, and 39-3.4.

cases.title: PCA v. Metropolitan New York Presbytery
cases.synopsis: The 51st General Assembly had cited Metropolitan New York Presbytery for persistence in error regarding its handling of a teaching elder who permitted a woman to preach from the pulpit. The SJC found that Presbytery's subsequent BCO 38-1 proceeding against TE Higgins (resulting in a censure of admonition) satisfied the deficiencies in the citation, concluding the 40-5 matter unanimously.

cases.title: Bulkeley & Gillikin v. Highlands Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: TE Craig Bulkeley and TE Skip Gillikin

precedent_refs: Session of Christ Covenant v. Central Carolina
cases.synopsis: TEs Bulkeley and Gillikin complained that Highlands Presbytery refused to record their dissent against Presbytery's act of recording a letter from the Arden Presbyterian Session affirming the Martins' Christian character. The SJC denied the complaint but went further, annulling Presbytery's recording of the Session letter as contrary to BCO 14-7 and the finality of the prior Sheppard decision (Case 2022-04), generating multiple concurring and dissenting opinions about the SJC's authority to reach issues not raised by the parties.

cases.title: Sears v. Nashville Presbytery
cases.synopsis: TE Ian Sears appealed his deposition by Nashville Presbytery, which handled the case as a BCO 38-1 case without process after he signed a written confession of guilt for two incidents of sexual misconduct in 2010 and 2011. The SJC unanimously denied the appeal on all five specifications of error, affirming the Presbytery's judgment and the validity of the BCO 38-1 process, including its procedures for drafting the confession and refusing to allow Sears to withdraw it after censure was imposed.
